revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:أمازاكي]][[bcl:Amazake]][[ca:Amazake]][[da:Amazake]][[de:Amazake]][[en:Amazake]][[eo:Amazake]][[es:Amazake]][[fr:Amazake]][[id:Amazake]][[it:Amazake]][[ja:甘酒]][[jv:Amazaké]][[ko:아마자케]][[ms:Amazake]][[no:Amazake]][[pt:Amazake]][[ru:Амадзакэ]][[tr:Amazake]][[uk:Амадзаке]][[zh:日本甘酒]]


{{short description|Japanese drink made from fermented rice|qq=}}
{{More citations needed|date=August 2021|qq=}} 
{{Italic title|reason=|qq=}}
{{Use dmy dates|date=February 2021|qq=}}
{{Infobox food
| name             = Amazake
| image            = Amazake by emily harbour in july.jpg
| image_size       = 250px
| caption          = A cup of ''amazake''
| alternate_name   = 
| country          = {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}
| region           = {{ill|East Asia|en|East Asia|qid=Q27231|lt=East Asia|1=East Asia}}
| national_cuisine = {{ill|Japanese cuisine|en|Japanese cuisine|qid=Q234138|lt=Japanese cuisine|1=Japanese cuisine}}
| creator          = [[Kofun period]] in Japan
| course           = {{ill|Drink|en|Drink|qid=Q40050|lt=Drink|1=Drink}}
| type             = {{ill|Plant milk|en|Plant milk|qid=Q2640574|lt=Plant milk|1=Plant milk}}
| served           = Warm, room temperature, or cold 
| main_ingredient  = Fermented {{ill|Rice|en|Rice|qid=Q5090|lt=Rice|1=Rice}}
| variations       = 
| calories         = 
| other            = 
}}
[[File:Amazake-from-supermarket-2019.jpg|thumb|260px|Several types of amazake from a supermarket]]
{{nihongo|'''''Amazake'''''|甘酒||extra={{IPA|ja|amazake||qq=}}|qq=}} is a traditional sweet, low-alcohol or non-alcoholic {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}ese drink made from fermented {{ill|Rice|en|Rice|qid=Q5090|lt=Rice|1=Rice}}. <ref>{{cite journal |last1=Goldbeck |first1=David |last2=Goldbeck |first2=NiCategoryi |date=October 1989 |title=Vegetarian Times |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=uQcAAAAAMBAJ&dq=Amazake&pg=PA65 |issue=146 |pages=65|qq=}}</ref> ''Amazake'' dates from the [[Kofun period]], and it is mentioned in the [[Nihon Shoki]].<ref>{{Cite web |last=Studarus |first=Laura |title=Uncovering amazake: Japan's ancient fermented 'superdrink' |url=https://www.bbc.com/travel/article/20200317-uncovering-amazake-japans-ancient-fermented-superdrink |access-date=2022-07-04 |website=www.bbc.com |language=en|qq=}}</ref> It is part of the family of traditional Japanese foods made using the [[:en:Koji (food)|koji mold]] {{nihongo|''{{ill|Aspergillus oryzae|en|Aspergillus oryzae|qid=Q22662068|lt=Aspergillus oryzae|1=Aspergillus oryzae}}''|麹|kōji|qq=}}, which also includes {{ill|Miso|en|Miso|qid=Q235169|lt=Miso|1=Miso}}, {{ill|Soy sauce|en|Soy sauce|qid=Q229385|lt=Soy sauce|1=Soy sauce}}, and [[sake]].<ref>Shurtleff, W.; Aoyagi. A. 1988. ''Amazake and Amazake Frozen Desserts''. Lafayette, California: Soyfoods Center. 69 + [52] pp.</ref><ref>{{cite book |last1=Shurtleff |first1=William |last2=Aoyagi |first2=Akiko |title=History of Koji – Grains and/or Soybeans Enrobed in a Mold Culture (300 BCE to 2021): Extensively Annotated Bibliography and Sourcebook |date=2021 |publisher=Soyinfo Center |location=Lafayette, CA |isbn=9781948436564 |url=https://www.soyinfocenter.com/pdf/265/Koji.pdf|qq=}}</ref>
There are several [[:en:recipes|recipes]] for ''amazake'' that have been used for hundreds of years. By a popular recipe, {{ill|Aspergillus oryzae|en|Aspergillus oryzae|qid=Q22662068|lt=''kōji''|1=Aspergillus oryzae}} is added to cooled whole grain rice causing {{ill|enzyme|en|enzyme|qid=Q8047|lt=enzyme|1=Enzyme}}s to break down the {{ill|Carbohydrate|en|Carbohydrate|qid=Q11358|lt=Carbohydrate|1=Carbohydrate}}s into simpler unrefined {{ill|sugar|en|sugar|qid=Q11002|lt=sugar|1=Sugar}}s. As the mixture incubates, sweetness develops naturally.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.mitoku.com/products/amazake/index.html|title=Amazake-Sweet Ambrosia|publisher=Mitoku|access-date=23 January 2010|archive-date=27 September 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200927041334/https://www.mitoku.com/products/amazake/index.html|url-status=dead|qq=}}</ref><ref name=":0">{{cite web |title=Amazake |url=http://japanesefood.about.com/od/japanesefoodglossary/g/Japanese_Amazake_Drink.htm |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110716053350/http://japanesefood.about.com/od/japanesefoodglossary/g/Japanese_Amazake_Drink.htm |archive-date=16 July 2011 |access-date=23 January 2010 |publisher=About.com|qq=}}</ref> By another recipe, {{ill|Sake kasu|en|Sake kasu|qid=Q987117|lt=Sake kasu|1=Sake kasu}} is mixed with water and sugar is added.<ref name=":0" /><ref>{{Cite web |title=おうちで簡単 酒粕で甘酒の作り方 作り方・レシピ |trans-title=Simple at-home recipe to make sake kasu amazake |url=https://www.kurashiru.com/recipes/7da37379-fe54-4e97-9f34-7dae42f17150 |access-date=2022-07-04 |website=クラシル |language=ja|qq=}}</ref>
''Amazake'' can be used as a {{ill|dessert|en|dessert|qid=Q182940|lt=dessert|1=Dessert}}, [[:en:snack food|snack]], natural {{ill|Sugar substitute|en|Sugar substitute|qid=Q626292|lt=sweetening agent|1=Sugar substitute}}, {{ill|Salad|en|Salad|qid=Q9266|lt=salad dressing|1=Salad}} or {{ill|smoothie|en|smoothie|qid=Q13290|lt=smoothie|1=Smoothie}}. One traditional ''amazake'' drink, prepared by combining ''amazake'' and water, heated to a simmer, and often topped with a pinch of finely grated [[:en:ginger root|ginger]], was popular with street vendors, and it is still served at inns, [[:en:Tea house|teahouses]], and at festivals. Many {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}}s and {{ill|Buddhist temples in Japan|en|Buddhist temples in Japan|qid=Q3141900|lt=Buddhist temples|1=Buddhist temples in Japan}} provide or sell it in the New Year.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2020-12-30 |title=正月に甘酒を飲むのはどうして？神社などで配られる理由 |trans-title=Why is amazake dRank on New Years? Here are the reasons why it is distributed by places such as shrines. |url=https://select-pharmacy.jp/goods/archives/8415 |access-date=2022-07-04 |website=SELECT PHARMACY（セレクトファーマーシー）|qq=}}</ref> In the 20th century, an instant version became available.
''Amazake'' contains many nutrients, including vitamin B1, B2, B6, folic acid, dietary fiber, oligosaccharide, cysteine, arginine and glutamine.<ref>{{cite book |last=Belleme |first=John |author2=Jan Belleme |title=Japanese Foods That Heal |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=lVz0tjE2NZAC&q=Amazake&pg=PA55 |access-date=2008-05-13 |year=2007 |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |isbn=978-0-8048-3594-7 |pages=55–58|qq=}}</ref> It is often considered a {{ill|hangover|en|hangover|qid=Q207334|lt=hangover|1=Hangover}} cure in Japan.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.bbc.com/travel/article/20200317-uncovering-amazake-japans-ancient-fermented-superdrink |first=Laura |last=Studarus |title=Uncovering amazake: Japan's ancient fermented 'superdrink' |work=BBC Travel |date=18 March 2020 |access-date=15 April 2023|qq=}}</ref> Outside Japan, it is often sold in Asian grocery stores during the winter months, and, all year round, in {{ill|natural food|en|natural food|qid=Q6980681|lt=natural food|1=Natural food}} stores in the U.S. and Europe, as a beverage and natural sweetener.
Similar beverages include the Chinese ''{{ill|jiuniang|en|jiuniang|qid=Q834563|lt=jiuniang|1=Jiuniang}}'' and Korean ''[[:en:Gamju|Gamju]]'' or ''{{ill|Sikhye|en|Sikhye|qid=Q701054|lt=Sikhye|1=Sikhye}}''. In grape winemaking, {{ill|must|en|must|qid=Q278818|lt=must|1=Must}} – sweet, thick, unfermented grape juice – is a similar product.
==See also==
{{Portal|Japan|Drink|qq=}}
* {{Annotated link |Akumochizake|qq=}}
* {{Annotated link |Brown rice syrup|qq=}}
*''{{ill|Choujiu|en|Choujiu|qid=Q2964598|lt=Choujiu|1=Choujiu}}''
*''[[:en:Gamju|Gamju]]'' – Korean equivalent of ''Amazake''
*''{{ill|Sikhye|en|Sikhye|qid=Q701054|lt=Sikhye|1=Sikhye}}'' – Korean equivalent of ''Amazake''
*''{{ill|jiuniang|en|jiuniang|qid=Q834563|lt=jiuniang|1=Jiuniang}}'' – Chinese equivalent of ''Amazake''
*{{ill|must|en|must|qid=Q278818|lt=must|1=Must}} – similar product in winemaking
* {{Annotated link |Rice milk|qq=}}
* {{Annotated link |Rice pu11ing|qq=}}
* {{Annotated link |Small beer|qq=}}
